# St. Hildegard

German mystic

Homework Help

Also known as: Hildegard of Bingen, Hildegard von Bingen, Si byl of the Rhine (Show More)

Written and fact-checked by

Mar. 5, 2026 • [History](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Hildegard/additional-info)

Britannica AI

Ask Anything

Table of Contents

Table of Contents Ask Anything

Top Questions

### Who is St. Hildegard?

St. Hildegard was a [Benedictine](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Benedictines) abbess, writer, poet, and composer who lived in 12th-century Germany. She had numerous prophetic and mystical visions during her life and is said to have been a [miracle](https://www.britannica.com/topic/miracle) worker.

### What was St. Hildegard’s childhood like?

Hildegard was born to noble parents in Böckelheim, West Franconia (Germany). She was a sickly child but was able to receive an education at a nearby [Benedictine](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Benedictines) cloister. She experienced her first religious visions at a young age and joined the nuns at age 15.

### Why is St. Hildegard famous?

St. Hildegard is one of the few prominent women in medieval church history. In fact, she is one of only four women who were named a [doctor of the church](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Doctor-of-the-Church) , meaning that her doctrinal writings have special authority in [Roman Catholicism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Roman-Catholicism) . She is considered by many to be a [patron saint](https://www.britannica.com/topic/patron-saint) of musicians and writers.

**St. Hildegard** (born 1098, Böckelheim, West Franconia [Germany]—died September 17, 1179, Rupertsberg, near Bingen; canonized May 10, 2012; feast day September 17) was a German [abbess](https://www.britannica.com/topic/abbess) , [visionary](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/visionary) [mystic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/mysticism) , and composer. In 2012 she was proclaimed a [doctor of the church](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Doctor-of-the-Church) , one of only four women to have been so named. She is revered as a [patron saint](https://www.britannica.com/topic/patron-saint) of musicians and writers.

[_(Read Britannica’s essay “Women of Faith: Meet the Four Female Doctors of the Church.”)_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Women-of-Faith-Meet-the-Four-Female-Doctors-of-the-Church)

Hildegard was born of noble parents and was educated at the [Benedictine](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Benedictines) cloister of Disibodenberg by Jutta, an anchorite (religious recluse) and sister of the count of Spanheim. Hildegard was 15 years old when she began wearing the Benedictine [habit](https://www.britannica.com/topic/religious-dress/Roman-Catholic-religious-dress) and pursuing a religious life. She succeeded Jutta as prioress in 1136.

Britannica Quiz World Religions & Traditions

Having experienced visions since she was a child, at age 43 she consulted her confessor, who in turn reported the matter to the [archbishop](https://www.britannica.com/topic/archbishop) of [Mainz](https://www.britannica.com/place/Mainz) . A committee of theologians subsequently confirmed the authenticity of Hildegard’s visions, and a [monk](https://www.britannica.com/topic/monk-monasticism) was appointed to help her record them in writing. The finished work, _[Scivias](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Scivias)_ (1141–52), consists of 26 visions that are prophetic and apocalyptic in form and in their treatment of such topics as the church, the relationship between God and humanity, and redemption. About 1147 Hildegard left Disibodenberg with several [nuns](https://www.britannica.com/topic/nun-monasticism) to found a new [convent](https://www.britannica.com/topic/convent) at Rupertsberg, where she continued to exercise the gift of [prophecy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/prophecy) and to record her visions in writing.

A talented poet and composer, Hildegard collected 77 of her [lyric poems](https://www.britannica.com/art/lyric) , each with a musical setting composed by her, in _Symphonia armonie celestium revelationum_ (“Symphony of the Harmony of Heavenly Revelations”). Her numerous other writings include lives of [saints](https://www.britannica.com/topic/saint) ; two [treatises](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/treatises) on medicine and natural history, reflecting a quality of scientific observation rare at that period; and extensive correspondence, in which are to be found further prophecies and allegorical treatises. For amusement, she contrived her own language. She traveled widely throughout [Germany](https://www.britannica.com/place/Germany) , evangelizing to large groups of people about her visions and religious insights.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/19154-050-891CD8D4/Universal-Man-manuscript-illumination-Scivias-Hildegard-of.jpg)

[_Universal Man_](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/19154-050-891CD8D4/Universal-Man-manuscript-illumination-Scivias-Hildegard-of.jpg) _Universal Man_ , manuscript illumination from _Scivias_ by Hildegard of Bingen. (more)

Her earliest biographer proclaimed her a [saint](https://www.britannica.com/topic/saint) , and [miracles](https://www.britannica.com/topic/miracle) were reported during her life and at her tomb. However, she was not formally [canonized](https://www.britannica.com/topic/canonization) until 2012, when Pope [Benedict XVI](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Benedict-XVI) declared her to be a saint through the process of “ equivalent canonization,” a papal proclamation of [canonization](https://www.britannica.com/topic/canonization) based on a standing tradition of popular veneration.

See all related content

As one of the few prominent women in [medieval](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/medieval) church history, Hildegard became the subject of increasing interest in the latter half of the 20th century. Her writings were widely translated into [English](https://www.britannica.com/topic/English-language) ; several recordings of her music were made available; and works of fiction, including Barbara Lachman’s _The Journal of Hildegard of Bingen_ (1993) and Joan Ohanneson’s _Scarlet Music: A Life of Hildegard of Bingen_ (1997), were published.

# mysticism

Homework Help

Also known as: mystical theology

Written by

Dan Merkur Psychoanalyst and Research Reader in the Department of the Study of Religion, Universtiy of Toronto. Author of _The Psychedelic Sacrament: Manna, Meditation, and Mystical Experience_ and _Mystical..._

Dan Merkur

Fact-checked by

Britannica Editors

Last updated

Feb. 27, 2026 • [History](https://www.britannica.com/topic/mysticism/additional-info)

Britannica AI

Ask Anything Quick Summary

Table of Contents

Table of Contents Quick Summary Ask Anything

Top Questions

* What is mysticism?
* How do mystics describe their experiences?
* What is the goal of mysticism?
* Which religions have traditions of mysticism?
* How do mystics try to reach spiritual understanding?
* What are some common symbols or practices found in different forms of mysticism?

**mysticism** , the practice of [religious](https://www.britannica.com/topic/religion) [ecstasies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ecstasy-religion) (religious experiences during [alternate](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/alternate) states of [consciousness](https://www.britannica.com/topic/consciousness) ), together with whatever [ideologies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ideology-society) , [ethics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ethics-philosophy) , rites, [myths](https://www.britannica.com/topic/myth) , [legends](https://www.britannica.com/art/legend-literature) , and [magic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/magic-supernatural-phenomenon) may be related to them.

The term _mystic_ is derived from the Greek noun _mystes_ , which originally designated an initiate of a secret cult or [mystery religion](https://www.britannica.com/topic/mystery-religion) . In Classical Greece (5th–4th century bce ) and during the [Hellenistic Age](https://www.britannica.com/event/Hellenistic-Age) (323 bce –330 ce ), the rites of the mystery religions were largely or wholly secret. The term _mystes_ is itself derived from the verb _myein_ (“to close,” especially the eyes or mouth) and signified a person who kept a secret. Early [Christianity](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Christianity) appropriated the technical vocabulary of the Hellenistic mysteries but later disavowed secrecy, resulting in a [transformation](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/transformation) of the meaning of _mystes_ . In subsequent Christian usage, _mystes_ , or _mystic_ , referred to practitioners of doctrinally acceptable forms of religious [ecstasy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ecstasy-religion) .

## The traditional conception of mysticism

From late antiquity through the [Middle Ages](https://www.britannica.com/event/Middle-Ages) , Christians used [prayer](https://www.britannica.com/topic/prayer) to contemplate both God’s omnipresence in the world and God in his essence. The [soul](https://www.britannica.com/topic/soul-religion-and-philosophy) ’s ecstasy, or rapture, in contemplation of God was termed a “spiritual marriage” by St. [Bernard of Clairvaux](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Bernard-of-Clairvaux) , the greatest mystical authority of the 12th century. In the 13th century the term _unio mystica_ (Latin: “mystical union”) came into use as a synonym. During the same period the range of objects of contemplation was increased to include the Passion of [Christ](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jesus) , visions of saints, and tours of [heaven](https://www.britannica.com/topic/heaven) and [hell](https://www.britannica.com/topic/hell) . In the 17th and 18th centuries the enthusiasms of [quaking](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Society-of-Friends) , [shaking](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shakers) , and other infusions of the [Holy Spirit](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Holy-Spirit) were also called mystical.

In the mid-19th century, after the [Romantic movement](https://www.britannica.com/art/Romanticism) had shifted the emphasis in much religious thinking from [theology](https://www.britannica.com/topic/theology) to individual experience, a growing interest in [ecumenism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ecumenism) led to the invention of the term _mysticism_ and its extension to comparable phenomena in non-Christian religions. The competition between the perspectives of theology and [science](https://www.britannica.com/science/history-of-science) resulted in a compromise in which most varieties of what had traditionally been called mysticism were dismissed as merely psychological phenomena and only one variety, which aimed at union with the Absolute, the [Infinite](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Infinite) , or God—and thereby the perception of its essential unity or oneness—was claimed to be genuinely mystical.

The historical evidence, however, does not support such a narrow [conception](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/conception) of mysticism. Even within the history of Christianity there were mystics—such as [Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Pseudo-Dionysius-the-Areopagite) in the 5th century and the anonymous author of the _[Cloud of Unknowing](https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Cloud-of-Unknowing)_ in the 14th—for whom the most desirable mystical experience or perception was not of unity but rather of nothing, or [nothingness](https://www.britannica.com/topic/emptiness) . Thus, St. [Bonaventure](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Bonaventure) , in addition to encouraging a program of mystical union with Christ in his death and resurrection, also recommended devotion to the wholly [transcendent](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/transcendent) nothingness of Pseudo-Dionysius’s theology. In the 14th century, [Meister Eckhart](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Meister-Eckhart) , along with his followers [Heinrich Suso](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Heinrich-Suso) , [Johann Tauler](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Johann-Tauler) , and [Jan van Ruysbroeck](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Jan-van-Ruysbroeck) , all sought experiences in which their souls disappeared, leaving only the [mind](https://www.britannica.com/topic/mind) , emotion, or the will of God. In the 17th century, St. [Teresa of Ávila](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Teresa-of-Avila) , almost certainly in ignorance of historical precedents, demoted the communion described by [St. Bernard](https://www.britannica.com/animal/Saint-Bernard-breed-of-dog) to the status of a “spiritual betrothal,” instead emphasizing the soul’s disappearance in the “spiritual marriage.”

The complexity of the historical record is multiplied exponentially when one includes other religious traditions in the survey. Both [Buddhism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Buddhism) and [Kabbala](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Kabbala) , the [esoteric](https://www.britannica.com/topic/esoteric) Jewish mysticism originating in the 12th century, [emphasize](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/emphasize) nothingness rather than oneness, and the notion of oneness itself has many varieties in both Christianity and Hinduism. These facts are inconsistent with the postulation of a single unity or oneness that mystics everywhere experience or perceive. It is not that the Absolute, the Infinite, or God is One, and mystics experience and perceive this truth. Rather, the data support a psychological interpretation regarding a tendency of the mind to unify its contents in different ways, resulting in slightly different experiences on different occasions. Mystics do not experience or perceive an objectively existing unity; rather they formulate their own experiential unities in different ways.

The traditional conception of mysticism was finally abandoned by academic scholars in the 1970s. Since then, some scholars have rejected the category of mysticism as a fiction, while others have enlarged it to [encompass](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/encompass) all religious uses of alternate states of [consciousness](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/consciousness) .

## Mysticism as experience and interpretation

## South Asian traditions

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/43154-050-DC7E9DED/Aspects-soma-sacrifice-behalf-Pune-ritual-India-500-bce.jpg)

[soma sacrifice](https://cdn.britannica.com/54/43154-050-DC7E9DED/Aspects-soma-sacrifice-behalf-Pune-ritual-India-500-bce.jpg) A soma sacrifice in Pune (Poona), India. (more)

Some mysticism can indeed be defined successfully in terms of the experience or perception of unity with the [divine](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sacred) . In [Hinduism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Hinduism) , for example, the hymns of the [Rig Veda](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Rigveda) were composed in [Sanskrit](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Sanskrit-language) by members of the Indo-European population that entered the northern plains of [India](https://www.britannica.com/place/India) from [Central Asia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Central-Asia) beginning about 1500 bce . In the [Vedic religion](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vedic-religion) the chief gods were [Indra](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Indra) , the king of the gods; [Agni](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Agni) , the fire god; and Soma, the god associated with the hallucinogenic [soma](https://www.britannica.com/topic/soma-Hinduism) plant. (The botanical identity of soma has been lost, but it was possibly the [fly agaric mushroom](https://www.britannica.com/science/fly-agaric) , _Amanita muscaria_ .) The major practice of Vedic religion was a sacrificial meal that the [community](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/community) shared with the gods, or [_deva_ s](https://www.britannica.com/topic/deva-religious-being) . Milk, clarified butter, curds, grains, the soma plant, and domestic animals were immolated in fire. The person who drank the hallucinogenic soma beverage was considered a [sacrifice](https://www.britannica.com/topic/sacrifice-religion) to the gods. He acquired intuitive, mystical insight and became one with the ancient [primordial](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/primordial) Man, who in the Vedic [creation myth](https://www.britannica.com/topic/creation-myth) had been divided into the many phenomena of the cosmos.

The Rig Veda contains other examples of mystical experience. One [hymn](https://www.britannica.com/topic/hymn) mentions long-haired [ascetics](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/ascetics) ( _kesin_ ) or silent ones ( _muni_ ), who were either naked or dressed in red. In their ecstasies they were “possessed by the gods” and able to fly outside their bodies. Their ecstasies were induced by a drug ( _siva_ ) that they consumed with the god [Rudra](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Rudra) . In post-Vedic times, Rudra was known as [Shiva](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shiva) , who was associated with the hallucinogenic plants of the genus [_Datura_](https://www.britannica.com/plant/Datura) .

A dramatic change in Indian mysticism is indicated in the Sanskrit texts known as the [Upanishads](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Upanishad) , which were composed between 600 and 300 bce . The _Maitri Upanishad_ outlined a practice of [Yoga](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Yoga-philosophy) (a practical and theoretical system of ancient [Indian philosophy](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Indian-philosophy) ) that consisted of breath control, the withdrawal of the senses (the voluntarily induced loss of sense perception), [meditation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/meditation-mental-exercise) , concentration, reasoning, and absorption. The goal of Upanishadic Yoga was to realize the identity of the personal self with the cosmic self, or _[atman](https://www.britannica.com/topic/atman)_ , and the identity of _atman_ with [_brahman_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/brahman-Hindu-concept) , or the divine essence. The divine essence in purest form was conceived as _vak_ (Sanskrit: “sound”); it developed secondarily into sacred sounds, such as AUM, and only thereafter into [coherent](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/coherent) words. Meditating on and uttering the sacred syllable were considered a means of inducing a mystical union with the divine essence. Once union was achieved, the self and all [existence](https://www.britannica.com/topic/existence) were seen to be divine. As noted in the _Brihadaranyka Upanishad_ , “Verily, by the seeing of, by the hearing of, by the thinking of, by the understanding of the Atman, all this [phenomenal world] is known.”

The _Yoga-sutra_ s, written by [Patanjali](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Patanjali) sometime between 200 bce and 400 ce , are the most [authoritative](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/authoritative) formulation of classical Yoga, which is also known as Raja (“Royal”) Yoga. Patanjali’s practice deleted the element of reasoning from Upanishadic Yoga while adding three preparatory components: self-restraint (from violence, falsehood, theft, incontinence, and acquisitiveness), commitment (to purity, contentment, austerity, self-study, and devotion to the Lord), and bodily postures. Doctrinally, Patanjali broke with the Vedic and Upanishadic belief that all existent things are a single substance that is God; he instead favored an uncompromising transcendentalism. He advised the practitioner (Yogi) to meditate one-pointedly on anything and everything. In each case, the Yogi would find that the object of meditation became the all, absorbed the self, and became evident as divine. Since different things were each the all, the self, and divine, it became logically necessary to [postulate](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/postulate) an unmanifest source or cause of the Yogic experience of absorption. A Yogi was consequently directed to meditate on the unmanifest that is beyond union. When the unmanifest was experienced contemplatively, it was found to be the soul or self ( [_purusha_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/purusha-Indian-philosophy) ; literally “spirit”) and was identified with a personified God ( [Ishvara](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Ishvara) ; “Lord”).

While pursuing these attainments, a Yogi would inevitably acquire one or more _siddha_ s (“powers”), such as knowledge of past lives, foreknowledge of one’s own death, great strength, supernormal senses, levitation, and omniscience. Although the _siddha_ s could be distractions from the goal of [_moksha_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/moksha-Indian-religion) (Sanskrit: “release”), or liberation from the cycle of death and rebirth ( _see_ [reincarnation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/reincarnation) ), they were recognized as positive acquisitions.

[_Bhakti_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/bhakti) (“devotion”), a religious movement that emerged in India in the 7th to 10th centuries, stressed love of the gods [Vishnu](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vishnu) and [Shiva](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shiva) and of the divine energy or goddess [Shakti](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shaktism) . Vishnu is conceptualized as _sat_ (Sanskrit: “being”), _cit_ (“consciousness”), and _ananda_ (“bliss”). Devotees of Vishnu, known as [Vaishnavites](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vaishnavism) , may practice Yoga in order to experience pure consciousness, which contains all things and is identified with Vishnu himself. In other instances, Vaishnavite _bhakti_ aims at more-limited [manifestations](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/manifestations) of Vishnu, such as a vision of his feet, his arms, or his smiling face. Devotion to Vishnu may also be directed to his [avatar](https://www.britannica.com/topic/avatar-Hinduism) (incarnation) [Krishna](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Krishna-Hindu-deity) . Meditations on the divinity at the source of all things, amid weeping, singing, and dancing, may aim at self-surrender to blissful possession by Krishna.

[](https://cdn.britannica.com/58/280058-050-A50C392F/hindu-diagram-of-chakras-tantrika-painting.jpg)

[Diagram of _chakra_ s](https://cdn.britannica.com/58/280058-050-A50C392F/hindu-diagram-of-chakras-tantrika-painting.jpg) Tantrika painting of the _chakra_ s in the body. (more)

Shiva is similarly conceptualized as pure consciousness, but in a fashion that [integrates](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/integrates) _bhakti_ with Yoga or with the [Tantric](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Tantra-religious-texts) (esoteric) religious practices of some Hindu, [Buddhist](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Buddhism) , and [Jaina](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Jainism) sects. Tantric mysticism may have originated historically as [ritual](https://www.britannica.com/topic/ritual) male copulation with women who were possessed by goddesses. Through the addition of Yoga, the divine marriage was internalized as an event within the Yogi. Tantric Yoga may be performed during [sexual intercourse](https://www.britannica.com/science/sexual-intercourse) or independently. In both events, Shiva is conceptualized as the pure consciousness that is all things. It is further held that the Yogi’s subtle body (a [metaphysical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/metaphysical) aspect of the person that is present at all times and visible in some dreams and alternate states) contains three vertical channels ( _nadi_ ) that pass through seven centers ( [_chakra_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/chakra) s; Sanskrit: “wheel”), from the genital regions to the top of the skull. A subtle energy, called [_kundalini_](https://www.britannica.com/topic/kundalini) and regarded simultaneously as Shakti and as the sperm of Shiva, is raised through Tantric Yoga practices along the central channel through the _chakra_ s. In the process, the practitioner rises together with the _kundalini_ , achieving immortality and acquiring magical powers.

In Tantric mysticism the body of the deity may be visualized as being composed of alphabetic letters or as assuming a specific posture—holding a jar of nectar or a book, for example. The mental image serves as a focus for the contemplation of pure consciousness, and a mystical reduction of experience to pure consciousness may ensue. The ultimate reducibility of everything to pure consciousness, belief in which is shared by [Vaishnavism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Vaishnavism) and [Shaivism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shaivism) , can be seen as a logical compromise between the Upanishadic union of all being and the Yogic quest for a unique transcendence of being. Hindu [mysticism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/mysticism) in its various forms identifies the unitive as [antithetical](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/antithetical) to the material world.
